## [16.0.0](https://github.com/Arize-ai/phoenix/compare/arize-phoenix-v15.12.0...arize-phoenix-v16.0.0) (2026-05-21)

[MIGRATION.md](https://github.com/Arize-ai/phoenix/blob/main/MIGRATION.md)


### ⚠ BREAKING CHANGES

* Sandboxing and Code Evaluators ([#13290](https://github.com/Arize-ai/phoenix/issues/13290))

### Features

Phoenix now lets you compose evaluation strategies in code.

Most eval tooling hands you a fixed menu of judge templates. Real evaluation is rarely that tidy.

Code Evaluators enable you to build evaluation criteria the way you want. You write a Python or TypeScript evaluate() function in the Phoenix UI — no SDK, no local runtime, no deploy step — and Phoenix runs it server-side, recording labels and scores as annotations on every experiment run.

Because it's just code, you control the whole strategy:

• Composite scoring: blend sub-scores (LLM judgment + deterministic rules) into one weighted metric
• Embedding-based evaluation: cosine similarity over embeddings instead of brittle string matching
• LLM juries: poll multiple models and combine verdicts into a weighted consensus

Sandboxed Code evaluators unlock the idea of agents as a j

Phoenix OAuth2 RBAC (`PHOENIX_OAUTH2_*_ROLE_ATTRIBUTE_PATH`, `GROUPS_ATTRIBUTE_PATH`) evaluates JMESPath against the **OIDC ID token and userinfo response only**, not the OAuth2 **access token** returned from the token endpoint.

Many identity providers follow OAuth/OIDC best practice and place authorization data (group membership, scopes, permissions) on the **access token**, not the ID token. Security teams often explicitly avoid putting authorization claims on ID tokens.

issues.title: Startup on version-sandboxes can block on WASM binary download
issues.body_excerpt:
## Problem

On the `version-sandboxes` branch, Phoenix startup can now depend on resolving or downloading the CPython WASM binary. The app unconditionally appends `prefetch_wasm_binary_if_needed` to `startup_callbacks_list`, and lifespan startup awaits each startup callback before yielding the app.

That means a fresh environment with no local WASM binary can block startup on `urllib.request.urlretrieve(...)`. The download path currently has no explicit timeout, so slow or blocked network access to GitHub can delay Phoenix from becoming ready. This also appears to run even when WASM is disable
issues.author: RogerHYang

Right now when you use PXI to perform any sort of annotation - it just adds ad-hoc annotations. This is okay except that if you perform this task over and over - you end up with a proliferation of differently named annotations. In addition the open-coding doesn't get added as notes.

- If PXI discovers a new axis / rubric it should codify it as an annotation config first BEFORE adding the annotation and associate it with the project so that when the agent re-visits the project it knows the already established grading criteria.

issues.title: Gemini playground calls don't capture tool calls in output messages
issues.body_excerpt:
## Description

When making a Gemini call from the Playground that results in a tool call, the tool call is not captured in the output messages of the trace. The trace shows only the assistant's text output, missing the tool call invocation.

This is inconsistent with the behavior of the Nova model, which does correctly capture tool calls in the output messages.

## Reproduction

## Summary

The PXI agent times out when run against `gpt-5.5`. The failure appears to be a latency/timeout issue rather than a logic error — likely caused by the model's extended reasoning, which pushes total response time past the current request timeout.

## Expected behavior

PXI should complete requests successfully on `gpt-5.5`, accommodating the additional latency introduced by reasoning models.

## Actual behavior

Requests to PXI on `gpt-5.5` time out before a response is returned.

`gpt-5.5` reasoning increases time-to-first-token / overall completion latency beyo

issues.title: Experiment runner: resource-aware admission for sandboxed evaluators
issues.body_excerpt:
## Background

PR #13364 caps concurrent Deno sandbox subprocesses with a module-level semaphore (cap 4), mirroring the WASM backend's `ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4)`. That fixes a real host-DoS, but it surfaces a deeper structural issue in how the experiment runner admits sandboxed evaluator work.

## The problem

There are **three independent scarce resources**, but the runner only has an admission gate for one of them:

| Resource | Real limit | Current gate |
|---|---|---|
| Phoenix host CPU/memory | ~host cores | none — WASM threads (4) and Deno slots (4) are bounded *inside* the work

issues.title: [agents] evaluator skill
issues.body_excerpt:
don't use llm as judge for everything.

When using llm as a judge, create a clear rubric as to how you want things to be evaluated. If the evaluator is overly complex, split the criteria into smaller judges.

Use simple binary classification if possible to make the judgement criteria clear and easy to use by humans. E.g. pass fail is better than something that produces a ton labels with un-clear action items.

Use code evaluators to add determinism, lean on simple heuristics when possible and the check can be encoded via simple built-in capabilities like regex, text search, etc.

```
discussions.body_excerpt:
We self-host Phoenix on Kubernetes backed by GCP Cloud SQL Postgres and are ingesting ~100k LLM interactions per day.

For our use case, it's important to be able to filter traces and spans by specific metadata attributes. For example, we would like to quickly find interactions belonging to a specific app and/or team.

We noticed that queries similar to the following are particularly slow, especially when searching for the past 24 hours or more:
```
SELECT
  spans.id,
  spans.start_time AS "startTime_span_sort_column"
FROM
  spans
JOIN
  traces
ON
  traces.id = spans.trace_rowid
